title: Financial Sentiment Dashboard
tagline: A live dashboard turning market headlines into sentiment signals — without the classic failure modes.

levels.label: Junior
levels.intro: Sentiment pipeline: why naive classifiers fail on financial text.
levels.theory: Financial sentiment analysis is harder than general sentiment analysis because: (1) Financial language is heavily domain-specific — 'the stock fell sharply' is negative, but 'the company beat expectations by falling below the predicted loss' is positive. General-purpose sentiment models trained on product reviews or social media will misclassify financial text at rates that make the output useless. (2) Entity-level sentiment matters — an article may be positive about Company A and negative about Company B. Sentence-level sentiment aggregated to article-level discards this structure entirely. (3) Temporal context — 'Q3 results disappoint' is negative at earnings time; the same phrase a year later in a retrospective may be neutral. Without temporal context, a classifier trained on historical data may misread current events. (4) Source matters — a Reuters wire report and a Twitter thread with 12 followers both produce text, but they carry vastly different signal. Weighting both equally produces noise.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Junior theory on domain-specific language — specifically the example that shows why general sentiment models fail on financial text.

levels.label: Senior
levels.intro: Real-time ingestion: rate limits, deduplication, and stale data detection.
levels.theory: A real-time financial sentiment dashboard has a data pipeline problem before it has an AI problem. (1) Deduplication — the same news story is syndicated to dozens of outlets within minutes. Without deduplication (by URL normalisation, content fingerprint, or headline fuzzy match), a single event appears to have 40x the sentiment signal it actually has. (2) Rate limits — financial data APIs (Polygon, Alpha Vantage, NewsAPI) have strict per-minute and per-day limits. A dashboard that fetches on every user page load will exhaust daily quotas within hours. Fetching must happen on a schedule, with results cached. (3) Stale data — displaying '2 hours ago' sentiment as 'current' during a fast-moving market event is actively misleading. The UI must surface data freshness prominently, and the dashboard must have a staleness threshold after which it stops displaying signals as 'live'. (4) Spike detection — a sudden 10x increase in article volume about a specific ticker is itself a signal and should be surfaced separately from sentiment direction.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Senior theory on deduplication — the scale of the problem (40x signal inflation) makes clear why this must be solved before sentiment analysis.

levels.label: Architect
levels.intro: Data integrity: conflicting signals, manipulation detection, and audit trails.
levels.theory: A financial dashboard that influences decisions must treat data integrity as a first-class concern. (1) Conflicting signals — it's common for sentiment signals to contradict each other (Reuters bullish, FT bearish, same hour). The dashboard must surface this conflict explicitly, not average it into a number that obscures the disagreement. (2) Coordinated inauthentic sentiment — financial social media manipulation is well-documented. A sudden burst of identical or near-identical positive sentiment from low-authority sources about a small-cap stock is a manipulation pattern, not genuine signal. Detection requires source diversity scoring and velocity analysis. (3) Audit trail — any dashboard that influences a financial decision must have an immutable record of what signal was shown to whom at what time. This is required for regulatory review and incident investigation. (4) Numerical precision — when displaying percentage changes or sentiment scores, the precision must match the signal quality. Displaying '73.4% positive' implies a precision the underlying classifier cannot support.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Architect theory on conflicting signals — specifically why averaging them is wrong and what to do instead.

levels.label: Executive
levels.intro: Regulatory exposure: investment advice boundaries, MiFID II, and safe harbour conditions.
levels.theory: A financial sentiment dashboard sits close to a regulated boundary. (1) Investment advice — displaying sentiment signals alongside price data and phrasing like 'bullish signal detected' may constitute investment advice under MiFID II (EU) or SEC rules (US), requiring authorisation you almost certainly don't have. The boundary between 'information' and 'advice' is not always clear and varies by jurisdiction. (2) Safe harbour conditions — to operate without authorisation, your dashboard must clearly and consistently present itself as information only, not recommendations; the user must make all investment decisions independently; and you must have documented evidence of this distinction. (3) Data vendor licensing — most financial data vendors prohibit redistribution or derivative products. Displaying sentiment derived from licensed news data may violate your data licence. Read the licence before building. (4) Market manipulation exposure — if your sentiment signal causes enough users to buy or sell simultaneously, you may inadvertently be participating in coordinated market activity, which carries regulatory risk even if unintentional.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Executive theory on the safe harbour conditions — specifically what 'documented evidence' of the distinction between information and advice means in practice.

tasks.scenario: You use a general-purpose sentiment model (trained on Amazon reviews and Twitter data) to classify financial news headlines. On the headline 'Company reports narrower-than-expected losses as cost-cutting takes hold', the model returns NEGATIVE. A finance professional reading this would say it's mildly POSITIVE. What's the cause?
options: The model is confused by headline length — financial headlines tend to be denser and shorter than the product reviews and tweets the model trained on, and shortening test inputs should fix the misclassification

The model has no financial domain knowledge — 'losses' and 'cost-cutting' are negative in general language, but in financial context the relevant signal is relative performance vs expectations and trajectory, not the presence of loss-associated words
The headline itself is genuinely ambiguous to any reader, financial professional or not, so no classifier — general-purpose or domain-specific — could be expected to label this one correctly either

The model just needs more training data — exposing it to a larger volume of financial headlines during fine-tuning will eventually teach it the correct sentiment regardless of the current architecture
hints: Think about what training data determines and what it can't teach a model. Re-read the Junior theory on domain-specific language.

The model correctly identifies 'losses' as negative in the context it was trained on. It has no way to know that in financial reporting, 'narrower-than-expected losses' is a positive surprise — that requires domain knowledge of how earnings are evaluated relative to analyst expectations.
tasks.why: General sentiment models are trained to associate words with sentiment in their training domain. 'Loss', 'cut', and 'narrow' are all negative in everyday language. A financial model knows these words in context: beating (even a loss) expectation is bullish; cost-cutting that improves trajectory is positive. You need a domain-specific model (FinBERT, or a model fine-tuned on financial text) or a prompt-based approach that provides the financial context explicitly.

tasks.scenario: This sentiment classifier runs at the article level and returns a single score. It loses entity-level information. Modify it to extract and score sentiment per named entity (company/ticker) mentioned in the text.

checks.label: Extracts named entities from the text
Scores sentiment per entity rather than per article

tasks.why: An article covering an earnings season can be positive about one company and negative about three others. Article-level sentiment hides this structure. Entity-level sentiment requires: (1) NER to extract company names and tickers; (2) sentence-level segmentation to associate sentences with the entities they mention; (3) sentiment classification of those entity-associated sentences. This is more expensive but produces the signal that's actually useful.

tasks.scenario: Put these steps of a financial sentiment pipeline in the correct order.
items: Run entity-level sentiment classification on entity-associated sentences
Fetch articles from news APIs with source authority weighting
Deduplicate by URL normalisation and content fingerprint
Extract named entities (companies, tickers) and associate sentences to entities
Aggregate entity sentiment scores, weighted by source authority and article recency
tasks.why: Fetch first, then deduplicate before any expensive processing — deduplication is cheap compared to NER and classification. NER and entity-sentence association come next because they structure the text for classification. Classification runs on the structured, deduplicated, entity-associated sentences. Aggregation is last because it requires all individual scores to be computed first.

tasks.scenario: You're building a sentiment score for AAPL. In the last hour you've processed 127 articles, of which 89 are syndicated copies of the same 3 Reuters and AP wire stories. Your dashboard shows '71% positive sentiment on 127 articles.' What's wrong with this number?
options: Nothing is wrong with this number — more articles is always more data, and more data is always a stronger basis for a sentiment estimate regardless of how many of those articles are duplicates of each other

The 127 article count is meaningless — you have sentiment from 3 unique sources with 124 copies inflating the apparent confidence. The real signal is n=3, and displaying n=127 implies a certainty the data doesn't support
The 71% figure itself is the problem — it's reported with too much precision, and simply rounding it down to 70% would resolve the apparent overconfidence in the number being displayed
The real problem here is the time window — a single hour is too short a sampling period for sentiment to be considered reliable, regardless of how many unique sources are actually represented in it
hints: Think about what the n=127 figure implies about the diversity of opinion vs what the data actually shows. Re-read the Senior theory on deduplication.
Statistical confidence in a proportion scales with the number of independent data points, not total data points. 124 copies of 3 articles is 3 independent data points with 124 duplicates.
tasks.why: Displaying n=127 implies 127 independent assessments of AAPL sentiment. The actual independent signal is 3 wire stories. Deduplication is essential before counting and aggregating — otherwise the apparent precision of the sentiment score is false. The dashboard should show: '71% positive based on 3 unique sources (127 total articles, 124 syndicated copies removed)'.

tasks.scenario: This dashboard fetches fresh data on every API call from the client. With 200 concurrent users, it will exhaust the NewsAPI daily quota in minutes. Fix it to fetch on a schedule and cache results.

checks.label: Implements caching or scheduled background fetching
Sets an expiry or staleness threshold on cached data

tasks.why: News APIs have daily request quotas. With 200 users each triggering a fetch, you exhaust a typical 1,000/day quota in 5 requests. The correct pattern: a background job fetches and processes data on a schedule (e.g. every 5 minutes), stores results in a cache with a TTL, and the API endpoint reads from the cache. Users get data that's at most 5 minutes old, and the API makes predictable, controlled requests.

tasks.scenario: Your dashboard is displaying TSLA sentiment from articles that are now 4 hours old. Elon Musk made a significant announcement 3 hours ago that shifted market opinion sharply. Your dashboard still shows the pre-announcement sentiment. What must your architecture do?
options: This is acceptable as-is — 4 hours falls comfortably within normal data staleness tolerances that are standard practice across financial sentiment dashboards in this kind of market

Surface data age prominently with a staleness threshold: if data is older than N minutes, display a clear 'data may be stale' warning and show the timestamp, so users can factor this into how they use the signal
The dashboard should automatically fetch fresh data the instant a user opens the page, overriding whatever is currently in the cache regardless of how recently it was last refreshed
Disable the dashboard entirely whenever the underlying data exceeds 1 hour old, to avoid any risk of a user making a decision based on a sentiment figure that isn't fully current
hints: Think about what displaying stale sentiment as 'current' implies to someone using it. Re-read the Senior theory on stale data.
The problem is not staleness per se — it's undisclosed staleness. A user who knows the data is 4 hours old can factor that in. A user who doesn't know is misled.
tasks.why: Staleness is a feature, not a bug, if it's disclosed. Display the timestamp of the most recent data on every widget, define and enforce a 'staleness threshold' (e.g. warn after 30 minutes, flag as potentially unreliable after 2 hours), and make the fetch schedule visible. Users in fast-moving markets can then decide whether to act on the signal or wait for fresher data.

tasks.scenario: Write the spike detection logic description for your dashboard's system prompt. Tell the model what constitutes a meaningful volume spike for a given ticker, how to distinguish organic from potentially coordinated spikes, and what action to take when one is detected.

checks.label: Defines spike relative to a baseline, not an absolute number
Specifies an action when a spike is detected

tasks.why: A spike is only meaningful relative to a baseline. 100 articles about AAPL is normal; 100 articles about a micro-cap is extraordinary. The baseline must be ticker-specific and time-adjusted. Coordinated spikes (many articles in a short window, low source diversity, similar language) are a different signal from organic spikes (diverse sources, varied language) and must be distinguished.

tasks.scenario: Your sentiment model returns a score of 0.734 positive for NVDA. Your dashboard displays this as '73.4% Bullish'. A user treats this as a precise signal and acts on it. What's the problem with displaying this level of precision?
options: Nothing is wrong with this — more decimal places in a displayed percentage simply communicate more information to the user, and there's no real downside to maximum precision in a dashboard reading

The precision of the display implies a precision the underlying model cannot support — sentiment classifiers have confidence intervals much wider than 0.1%, and displaying 73.4% suggests the model can distinguish 73.4% from 73.1%, which it cannot
73.4% is simply a confusing number for users to parse at a glance — rounding the display to the nearest 5%, like 75%, would make it easier to read without changing what it actually represents

The real issue is the label 'Bullish' rather than the number itself — sentiment direction and market price direction are conceptually different things and shouldn't be conflated in the same UI element
hints: Think about what 73.4% implies about the model's measurement precision vs what a sentiment classifier can actually measure. Re-read the Architect theory on numerical precision.
A classifier trained on financial text might have inter-rater agreement of 80-85% on the underlying training labels. The model's output certainty cannot exceed the precision of the training signal.
tasks.why: Displaying raw model output scores as percentages implies false precision. A sentiment classifier with 85% label agreement cannot produce a score that's meaningful to two decimal places. Display: the direction (bullish/bearish/neutral), a confidence band (high/medium/low or a range like 65-80% positive), and always show the n (based on N articles). Never imply precision the model cannot support.

tasks.scenario: Your dashboard shows TSLA: 68% Bullish (Reuters, FT, Bloomberg). Simultaneously it also has TSLA: 71% Bearish (WSJ, Barron's, Seeking Alpha). Both signals are from the last 2 hours. What should the dashboard display?
options: Average the two signals together and display TSLA at 51% Bullish overall, since combining all available source sentiment into one net figure is the standard way to summarise a single ticker's outlook
Display only the higher-authority signal — Reuters, FT, and Bloomberg — and quietly discard the WSJ and Barron's signal, since established wire services are generally considered more reliable sources
Surface the conflict explicitly: show both signals, the sources behind each, and a 'conflicting signals' indicator — averaging or hiding the conflict destroys the information

Display 'no signal available' for TSLA in this window — when two reputable source groups disagree this sharply, the safest response is to withhold any sentiment reading until the disagreement resolves
hints: Think about what information the conflict itself carries and what averaging would destroy. Re-read the Architect theory on conflicting signals.
The conflict is the signal. When authoritative sources disagree, that's important information for a decision-maker — it means the situation is genuinely uncertain, not that the average is the truth.
tasks.why: Averaging conflicting signals destroys information. If Reuters (68% bullish) and WSJ (71% bearish) disagree on the same stock in the same 2-hour window, the correct signal is 'there is genuine uncertainty and disagreement among authoritative sources.' Surface both signals, their source composition, and the conflict. A decision-maker can act on 'sources are divided' more usefully than on an averaged '51% bullish' that obscures the disagreement.

tasks.scenario: Your dashboard has no audit trail. Add logging that records what sentiment signal was displayed, to which user session, at what time, so you have an immutable record for regulatory review.

checks.label: Records when the signal was served
Indicates the log is append-only or immutable

tasks.why: Regulatory review of financial dashboards requires being able to reconstruct exactly what was shown to whom at what time. An audit log must be: (1) written before the response is returned (not after); (2) append-only — no updates or deletes; (3) include: timestamp, user identifier, ticker, signal values, data freshness at time of serving. Logging to a mutable database table that can be updated fails the immutability requirement.

tasks.scenario: Write the manipulation detection logic for your dashboard. In 2-3 sentences, describe: what pattern constitutes a potentially coordinated sentiment signal, what you do when you detect it, and how you surface it to the user.
tasks.placeholder: A potentially coordinated sentiment signal is detected when...

checks.label: Defines the manipulation pattern in terms of source diversity or content similarity
States what happens to the potentially coordinated signal
States how the user is informed

tasks.why: Manipulation detection must be specific and operational. 'Many articles from few sources in a short time with similar language' is the core pattern. The detected signal should be quarantined from the main sentiment calculation and surfaced separately with a 'potential coordinated activity' label — not silently discarded (which hides information) and not mixed into the main signal (which corrupts it).

tasks.scenario: A source you've assigned high authority (financial blog with 50k subscribers) publishes a strongly bullish article on a small-cap stock. Your authority scoring is based on subscriber count and domain age. Three hours later you discover the blog is operated by an investor with an undisclosed long position. What does this reveal about your authority model?
options: Nothing is revealed here about the authority model itself — this is simply an isolated edge case involving one bad actor, and the underlying scoring methodology based on subscriber count remains sound

Authority based on reach metrics (subscribers, domain age) conflates audience size with trustworthiness — a disclosed conflict of interest should reduce authority to near-zero regardless of subscriber count, and your model has no conflict-of-interest detection
You should exclude financial blogs from your source pool entirely going forward and rely exclusively on wire services like Reuters and AP, since blogs as a category carry this risk inherently
Add a manual review step specifically for any article covering small-cap stocks, since the size of the company being discussed is the actual factor that should trigger additional scrutiny here
hints: Think about what 'authority' actually means for signal quality vs what subscriber count measures. Re-read the Junior theory on source weighting.

Subscriber count measures reach, not trustworthiness. A widely-read source with a conflict of interest is a more dangerous signal than a low-reach source with a conflict, because more people act on it.
tasks.why: Authority for financial signal purposes must measure trustworthiness and editorial independence, not audience size. Subscriber count and domain age are proxies for reach. A conflict-of-interest scoring dimension — checking for disclosed interests, analysing language patterns that correlate with undisclosed pumping — is a necessary part of authority scoring in financial contexts. This is unsolved in most commercial implementations.

tasks.scenario: Your dashboard displays 'Strong Bullish Signal' for a stock. A user buys £50,000 of that stock, it falls 30% in a week, and they claim the dashboard constituted investment advice. Under MiFID II, what determines whether their claim has merit?
options: The claim has no merit at all — your platform included disclaimer language stating results are not investment advice, and that disclaimer is sufficient on its own to settle any dispute of this kind
The claim has merit if the signal was presented in a way a reasonable person would interpret as a recommendation, regardless of disclaimers — substance matters more than the label attached
The claim only has merit if the £50,000 trade size exceeds some specific regulatory threshold for what counts as a material investment decision under MiFID II's definition of investment advice
The claim only has merit if your platform charged the user a subscription fee for dashboard access — free-to-use information services are generally exempt from MiFID II's investment advice provisions
hints: Think about what MiFID II looks at — the substance of what was communicated, or the disclaimer attached to it. Re-read the Executive theory on the investment advice boundary.

Disclaimers reduce but do not eliminate regulatory exposure. Regulators look at the substance of the communication — would a reasonable person interpret this as a recommendation? — not just the label attached to it.
tasks.why: Under MiFID II, the investment advice boundary is determined by the substance of the communication, not its label. 'Strong Bullish Signal' displayed prominently with a chart, price target context, and a buy-facilitating link is substantively closer to a recommendation than to neutral information, regardless of disclaimer text. Safe harbour requires the communication to be genuinely informational in substance — presenting objective data without directional characterisation — not just labelled as such.

tasks.scenario: Write the safe harbour language that must appear on every page of your financial sentiment dashboard to establish that it provides information, not advice. It must be specific enough to be defensible under MiFID II, not generic disclaimer boilerplate.
tasks.placeholder: This dashboard provides aggregated sentiment analysis of publicly available news sources...

checks.label: Explicitly states this is not investment advice
Directs the user to make their own decision or seek professional advice
States a limitation on the completeness or reliability of the data

tasks.why: Safe harbour language must be specific and prominent to be defensible. Generic 'not financial advice' footers in 8pt font have been ruled insufficient. The language must clearly state: what the product is (aggregated sentiment data), what it is not (personalised advice or a recommendation), what the user must do (apply independent judgment or consult a professional), and the data's limitations (based on available sources, may be stale, may be incomplete).

tasks.scenario: Your sentiment dashboard is licensed to use news data from a major aggregator. The licence says: 'Data may be used for internal analysis. Redistribution or creation of derivative works for commercial distribution requires a separate licence.' You want to sell access to your dashboard. Which of the following requires the separate licence?
options: None of these require the separate licence — sentiment scores you generate are your own original analysis output, not a redistribution of the underlying licensed articles in any meaningful sense

All of them — a dashboard that displays sentiment derived from licensed news data and is sold commercially is a derivative work for commercial distribution, regardless of how the source data is transformed

Only displaying the original article headlines alongside your sentiment scores would trigger the need for a separate licence — the numerical sentiment scores themselves don't constitute redistribution
Only if your customer base exceeds 100 paying users would this require the separate licence — smaller-scale commercial deployments are typically covered under standard internal-use licence terms
hints: Think about whether transforming the data (into sentiment scores) breaks the licence chain. Re-read the Executive theory on data vendor licensing.

Licence terms in financial data are drafted broadly. 'Derivative works' is a legal term that typically covers transformed versions of the original data — sentiment scores derived from licensed articles are a derivative work.
tasks.why: Financial data licences are drafted to capture derivative value, not just raw redistribution. A sentiment score derived from licensed news content and sold commercially is a derivative work for commercial distribution in standard licence language. You need to read the actual licence and in most cases get a written clarification from the vendor before building a commercial product on licensed data. Verbal assurances are not binding.

tasks.scenario: Your dashboard has 50,000 active users. You publish a 'Sentiment Surge' alert when a stock's sentiment score moves more than 20% in 30 minutes. Multiple users have configured automated trading systems to buy on Surge alerts. You haven't built these automations — users did. What's your regulatory exposure?
options: None — you're not the one executing trades, your users are, and a platform that simply surfaces a signal isn't responsible for the downstream automated systems individual users choose to build on top of it

Potential exposure as a de facto signal service contributing to coordinated market activity — if alerts reliably trigger simultaneous trading at this scale, intent doesn't determine the regulatory question

Exposure only exists here if you have specific knowledge that particular users are running automated trading systems against your alerts — without that direct knowledge, your platform carries no additional risk

There's no exposure in this scenario because your platform provides information rather than executing trades directly — that distinction alone is generally sufficient to place you outside market manipulation rules
hints: Think about the effect of your signals at scale, not just the intent. Re-read the Executive theory on market manipulation exposure.
At 50,000 users with automated systems, your signal is functionally a trade trigger for a significant volume of coordinated activity. The regulatory question is about effect, not intent.
tasks.why: Regulatory exposure in financial markets is often determined by effect, not intent. A signal service whose alerts reliably trigger simultaneous coordinated activity across a large user base may be treated as a market-moving participant regardless of whether trades are executed by humans or algorithms the service didn't build. At 50,000 users, you need legal counsel before publishing time-sensitive alerts that could be — and are — used as automated trade triggers.
